name: Unnamed aqueduct at Ephesus (Aqua Iulia?)

props.description: The longest and most recently-constructed Roman aqueduct in the province of Asia, measuring 36.5 km from its source in the hills south of Ephesus to the metropolis itself. The aqueduct was first commissioned by the emperor Hadrian in 122-126, and it portions of it remained in use into the medieval period. In modern times, it is known as the Değirmendere Aqueduct, after the nearby Kenchrios river (modern Değirmendere).

name: Wādī Ki‘ām Aqueduct (Lepcis)

props.description: A Roman aqueduct, probably built during the reign of Hadrian (or possibly Trajan), that brought water approximately 20km from Wādī Ki‘ām to Lepcis Magna, where it is thought to have fed a series of reservoirs, cisterns, and settling tanks prior to distribution to urban fountains and baths. Remains of contemporary dams and aqueduct components paralleling the wadi are thought to have been involved in collection for the aqueduct; the route from there is thought to have been a straight line from the collection apparatus to the city.

name: Roman spring sanctuary near Heckenmünster

props.description: An extensive complex of structures dating to the 1st - 3rd centuries of the Roman imperial period sited at a pair of springs known today as "Viktoriaquelle" and "Schwefelquelle," near modern Heckenmünster in German. This site, whose ancient name and tutelary deities are not known, was extensively excavated in the 1960s. It comprised religious and performative spaces, as well as baths and hostelry.
